subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 16! In this unit you will become an expert on chance – the special part of maths that helps us talk about how likely something is to happen. Chance is all around you, every single day. When Amina wonders whether it will rain before she reaches the masjid, when Yusuf flips a bottle cap to decide who kicks off the football match, or when Halima guesses which goat will reach the water trough first, they are all thinking about chance.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, run a pretend coin-flip experiment with you, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

As you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and – if you can – a coin and a die beside you. Chance is a subject you learn best by doing it. Flip the coin, roll the die, and watch what happens. If a result surprises you, that is wonderful – surprises are how we discover that chance does not always do what we expect. Every scientist who studies chance once sat exactly where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
Use the words certain, likely, even chance, unlikely and impossible correctly.
Place events on a probability scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).
Write a simple probability as a fraction, and know it can also be a decimal or percentage.
Work out the probability of an event using favourable outcomes ÷ total outcomes.
Compare two events using more likely than, less likely than and equally likely as.
Tell the difference between a fair experiment and an unfair (biased) one.
Carry out a chance experiment, record results neatly, and find the experimental probability.
Predict how many times an event should happen using probability × number of trials.

concepts.title: The Language of Chance
concepts.explanation: Chance is a way of describing how likely something is to happen. Before we use any numbers at all, we describe chance with words. There are five words that matter most in this unit, and you should learn them like old friends. Certain – it will definitely happen. There is no doubt at all.
concepts.example: Likely – it will probably happen, but we cannot be completely sure.

concepts.title: The Probability Scale from 0 to 1
concepts.explanation: Words are useful, but mathematicians also like to measure chance with numbers. We do this on a special number line called the probability scale. It runs from 0 to 1, and every chance in the world fits somewhere on it. 0 means the event is impossible. It has no chance at all.
concepts.example: 1 means the event is certain. It will definitely happen.

concepts.title: Working Out Simple Probabilities
concepts.explanation: When every outcome is equally likely, we can work out an exact probability with a simple rule. This is one of the most useful ideas in the whole unit, so read it slowly: ⭐ The Probability Rule Probability = number of favourable outcomes divided by total number of possible outcomes. A favourable outcome is simply the result we are hoping for or asking about.
concepts.example: Let us see the rule in action with a six-sided die. A die has six faces: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6. That means there are 6 possible outcomes in total, and on a fair die each is equally likely. Now we can answer many questions:

concepts.title: Comparing How Likely Events Are
concepts.explanation: Often we do not need an exact fraction – we just want to know which of two events is more likely. To compare events we use three handy phrases: More likely than – this event has a bigger chance than that one.
concepts.example: Less likely than – this event has a smaller chance than that one.

concepts.title: Fair and Unfair Chance Experiments
concepts.explanation: A chance experiment is any activity with more than one possible result, where you cannot say for sure what will happen each time. Flipping a coin, rolling a die, spinning a spinner, and drawing a marble from a bag are all chance experiments. The single results – heads, or a 4, or a red marble – are called outcomes, and each go of the experiment is called a trial. Experiments can be fair or unfair. In a fair experiment, every outcome has an equal chance of happening. A fair coin gives heads and tails each a…
concepts.example: In an unfair (also called biased) experiment, some outcomes are more likely than others. A coin that has been bent might land on heads far more often than tails. A spinner with one huge section and three tiny sections…

concepts.title: Experimental Probability
concepts.explanation: So far we have worked out chances by thinking – this is called theoretical probability, because it comes from the theory, or the idea, of the experiment. But we can also find a probability by actually doing the experiment many times and counting what happens. This is called experimental probability. ⭐ Experimental Probability Rule Experimental probability = number of times the outcome happened divided by the total number of trials. The more trials you do, the closer your experimental probability usually gets to…
concepts.example: Suppose Maryam flips a coin 20 times and counts 11 heads and 9 tails. Her experimental probability of heads is 11 out of 20. The theoretical probability of heads is one half, which would be 10 out of 20. Her result of…

explorations.title: The Language of Chance
explorations.context: Chance is a way of describing how likely something is to happen. Before we use any numbers at all, we describe chance with words. There are five words that matter most in this unit, and you should learn them like old friends. Certain – it will definitely…

explorations.answer: 1) a) certain, b) even chance, c) impossible, d) likely.
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

explorations.answer: a) one sixth, b) 4 out of 10 = two fifths, c) one half.

explorations.answer: 1) a) one sixth, b) 3 out of 6 = one half, c) 2 out of 6 = one third, d) 6 out of 6 = 1 (certain).

methods.title: How to work out a probability
methods.example: Which word best describes this event: 'A normal die will land on a number from 1 to 6'?
steps: Count the favourable outcomes (the results you want).
Count the total number of possible outcomes.
Write the fraction: favourable over total.
Simplify the fraction if you can (3 out of 6 becomes one half).

methods.title: How to find an experimental probability
methods.example: Where does 'A flipped coin lands on tails' sit on the probability scale?
steps: Do the experiment many times and tally the results.
Count how many times your outcome happened.
Write it over the total number of trials.
Remember: more trials usually give a result closer to the theoretical probability.

methods.title: How to make a prediction
methods.example: What is the probability of rolling an odd number on a fair six-sided die?
steps: Find the probability of the event as a fraction.
Multiply that fraction by the number of trials.
State the answer as 'about' – the real result will wobble around it.

methods.title: A marble bag
methods.example: A bag has 4 red, 3 blue and 3 yellow marbles. What is the probability of drawing a blue marble?
steps: Solution: First find the total: 4 + 3 + 3 = 10 marbles.
Blue marbles are favourable: there are 3 of them.
So the probability is 3 out of 10 (three tenths).

methods.title: The 'not' shortcut
methods.example: Using the same bag (4 red, 3 blue, 3 yellow), what is the probability of drawing a marble that is not red?
steps: Solution: The probability of red is 4 out of 10.
Something must happen, so the whole is 1, which is 10 out of 10.
Take red away: 10 − 4 = 6.
So 'not red' is 6 out of 10, which simplifies to three fifths.

methods.title: Compare two events
methods.example: Which is more likely on a fair die: rolling a 2, or rolling a number greater than 4?
steps: Solution: Rolling a 2 is 1 out of 6.
Numbers greater than 4 are 5 and 6, so that is 2 out of 6.
Both have the same bottom number, and 2 is bigger than 1, so rolling a number greater than 4 is more likely.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right chance word
workedExamples.prompt: Which word best describes this event: 'A normal die will land on a number from 1 to 6'?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Every face of a die shows a number from 1 to 6, so this event always happens. It is certain, with a probability of 1. There is no way for the die to land on anything else.

workedExamples.title: Place an event on the scale
workedExamples.prompt: Where does 'A flipped coin lands on tails' sit on the probability scale?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A coin has two equally likely outcomes, heads and tails. Tails is one of the two, so its probability is one half. That sits exactly in the middle of the scale – an even chance.

workedExamples.title: Work out a die probability
workedExamples.prompt: What is the probability of rolling an odd number on a fair six-sided die?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The odd numbers on a die are 1, 3 and 5 – that is 3 favourable outcomes. There are 6 outcomes in total. So the probability is 3 out of 6, which simplifies to one half. Rolling an odd number is an even chance.

workedExamples.title: A marble bag
workedExamples.prompt: A bag has 4 red, 3 blue and 3 yellow marbles. What is the probability of drawing a blue marble?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First find the total: 4 + 3 + 3 = 10 marbles. Blue marbles are favourable: there are 3 of them. So the probability is 3 out of 10 (three tenths).

workedExamples.title: The 'not' shortcut
workedExamples.prompt: Using the same bag (4 red, 3 blue, 3 yellow), what is the probability of drawing a marble that is not red?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The probability of red is 4 out of 10. Something must happen, so the whole is 1, which is 10 out of 10. Take red away: 10 − 4 = 6. So 'not red' is 6 out of 10, which simplifies to three fifths. (Check: blue 3 + yellow 3 = 6. It matches!)

workedExamples.title: Compare two events
workedExamples.prompt: Which is more likely on a fair die: rolling a 2, or rolling a number greater than 4?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Rolling a 2 is 1 out of 6. Numbers greater than 4 are 5 and 6, so that is 2 out of 6. Both have the same bottom number, and 2 is bigger than 1, so rolling a number greater than 4 is more likely.

workedExamples.title: Experimental probability
workedExamples.prompt: Khadija flipped a coin 50 times and got 28 heads and 22 tails. What is the experimental probability of heads?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Heads happened 28 times out of 50 trials, so the experimental probability is 28 out of 50. This is a little more than one half (which would be 25 out of 50), but close – exactly what we expect from a fair coin over 50 flips.

workedExamples.title: Make a prediction
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner has 4 equal sections: red, blue, green and yellow. If you spin it 40 times, about how many times should it land on blue?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Blue is 1 of the 4 equal sections, so the probability of blue is one quarter. Expected times = one quarter of 40 = 10 times. You might get 8 or 12 in real life, but about 10 is what to expect.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: The chance of an event is three tenths. What is the chance it does not happen?
workedExamples.solution: does not happen = 1 − three tenths = seven tenths.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner has 4 equal sections: red, red, blue, yellow. What is the probability of landing on red?

workedExamples.title: Say whether each experiment is fair or unfair
workedExamples.prompt: Say whether each experiment is fair or unfair: a) a coin that lands heads 9 times out of 10, b) a normal six-sided die, c) a spinner with equal sections.
workedExamples.solution: a) unfair (biased), b) fair, c) fair.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A fair die is rolled 60 times. Predict about how many times each number should appear, and explain why the real result might be different.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Each number: probability one sixth, so expect 60 ÷ 6 = 10 times each; real results wobble because chance varies from trial to trial.

practice.answer: 1) a) certain, b) even chance, c) impossible, d) likely.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: A fair die is rolled. Find the probability of: a) a 4, b) an odd number, c) a number greater than 4, d) a number less than 7.
practice.answer: 1) a) one sixth, b) 3 out of 6 = one half, c) 2 out of 6 = one third, d) 6 out of 6 = 1 (certain).
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: A bag has 6 red, 3 blue and 1 green marble. Find the probability of drawing: a) red, b) blue, c) green, d) not blue.
practice.answer: total 10 marbles: a) red 6 out of 10 = three fifths, b) blue 3 out of 10, c) green 1 out of 10, d) not blue 7 out of 10.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: Compare using more likely / less likely / equally likely as: a) rolling a 6 ___ rolling a number less than 3; b) drawing red ___ drawing blue from a bag of 5 red and 5 blue.
practice.answer: a) rolling a 6 (one sixth) is less likely than a number less than 3 (2 out of 6 = one third); b) red is equally likely as blue.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: The chance of an event is three tenths. What is the chance it does not happen?
practice.answer: does not happen = 1 − three tenths = seven tenths.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: A spinner has 4 equal sections: red, red, blue, yellow. What is the probability of landing on red?
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: Say whether each experiment is fair or unfair: a) a coin that lands heads 9 times out of 10, b) a normal six-sided die, c) a spinner with equal sections.
practice.answer: a) unfair (biased), b) fair, c) fair.
practice.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

practice.prompt: A fair die is rolled 60 times. Predict about how many times each number should appear, and explain why the real result might be different.
practice.answer: 1) Each number: probability one sixth, so expect 60 ÷ 6 = 10 times each; real results wobble because chance varies from trial to trial.
practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: The Coin-Flip Experiment
activities.materials: a coin, paper and a pencil.
steps: Before you start, predict: how many heads do you think you will get in 20 flips?
Flip the coin 20 times. Make a tally mark for heads or tails each time.
Count your heads and tails. Write the experimental probability of heads as 'heads out of 20'.
Compare it to the theoretical probability of one half (which would be 10 out of 20). How close were you?
If you have time, do another 20 flips and add them to the first. With 40 flips, is your result closer to one half? This is the law that more trials give a more reliable answer.

activities.title: Roll and Tally (Die Investigation)
activities.materials: a die, paper and a pencil.
steps: Draw a tally chart with a row for each number 1 to 6.
Roll the die 30 times, making a tally mark for each result.
Count the tallies. Which number came up most? Which came up least?
The theoretical chance of each number is one sixth (about 5 times in 30). How do your results compare?
Ask yourself: does this mean the die is unfair, or is this just the normal wobble of chance? Discuss your idea with a family member.

activities.title: Make a Marble Bag
activities.materials: a bag or cup, and 10 small objects in different colours (beans, stones or bottle caps).
steps: Put in 5 of one colour, 3 of another, and 2 of a third. Write down how many of each.
Work out the theoretical probability of drawing each colour as a fraction.
Draw one object without looking, record its colour, then put it back. Repeat 20 times.
Compare your experimental results to the fractions you calculated. Are they close?

activities.title: Build a Spinner and Test Fairness
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a small stick or matchstick for the pointer.
steps: Draw a circle and divide it into 4 equal sections. Colour them red, blue, green and yellow.
Push the stick through the centre so it can spin.
Predict the chance of landing on each colour, then spin 40 times and tally the results.
Now draw an *unfair* spinner with one big section and three tiny ones. Predict how the results will differ, then test it.

activities.title: Chance Word Sort (Family Game)
activities.materials: small slips of paper and a pencil.
steps: On five slips, write: certain, likely, even chance, unlikely, impossible. Lay them out in order.
Each player thinks of a real event and says it aloud – for example, 'It will rain today.'
Everyone points to the slip that best matches the event, and you discuss any disagreements.
Keep score: a point for each event you and a partner label the same way.

activities.title: Prediction Challenge (Investigation)
activities.materials: a die, paper and a pencil, and other people if possible.
steps: Predict how many times you will roll a 6 in 30 rolls. (Hint: the chance of a 6 is one sixth.)
Roll the die 30 times and count your 6s. Compare to your prediction.
If you have a group, add everyone's rolls together. With, say, 150 rolls in total, how many 6s appeared?
Is the big group's result closer to the prediction than your own 30 rolls? Talk about why.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand chance best? Were your experimental results close to the theoretical probabilities, or did they wobble? What surprised you most? Why do more trials give a more reliable answer? Share your thoughts with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: The Probability Scale 0 = impossible. 1 = certain. One half = even chance. Every probability is between 0 and 1 – never less than 0, never more than 1.

rules.title: The Probability Rule
rules.text: Probability = favourable outcomes divided by total outcomes. Example: rolling an even number on a die = 3 out of 6 = one half.

rules.title: The 'Not' Rule
rules.text: Probability an event does NOT happen = 1 minus the probability it does. If rain is one quarter, then no rain is three quarters.

rules.title: The Prediction Rule
rules.text: Expected number of times = probability multiplied by number of trials. Example: a 4 on a die over 60 rolls = one sixth of 60 = about 10 times.

terms: How likely something is to happen
Definitely will / definitely will not happen
Just as likely to happen as not (one half)
One possible result of an experiment
Outcomes equally likely / some more likely (biased)
One go of a chance experiment
commonMistakes: Unlikely can still happen; impossible never can

realProblems.prompt: At the Hargeisa market, a prize barrel holds 15 slips: 3 win a mango, 12 win nothing. Farah draws one slip. What is the probability she wins a mango? Describe this chance in words.
realProblems.answer: 1) 3 out of 15 = one fifth; this is unlikely (a low chance).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 3 out of 15 = one fifth; this is unlikely (a low chance).

realProblems.prompt: The forecast gives a one-third chance of rain each day for 9 days. About how many rainy days should Yusuf expect? Could it rain on all 9 days? Explain.
realProblems.answer: Expect one third of 9 = 3 rainy days; yes, rain on all 9 days is possible but very unlikely, because each day is only a chance.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Expect one third of 9 = 3 rainy days; yes, rain on all 9 days is possible but very unlikely, because each day is only a chance.

realProblems.prompt: Omar has 20 goats and picks one at random to sell at Berbera. If 5 of the goats are brown, what is the probability he picks a brown goat? Is that more or less than an even chance?
realProblems.answer: 5 out of 20 = one quarter, which is less than an even chance.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 out of 20 = one quarter, which is less than an even chance.

realProblems.prompt: A class of 30 pupils puts their names in a hat for a football captain draw. What is the probability that Liban is chosen? If the draw were repeated every week for a term, would Liban definitely be chosen at some point? Explain.
realProblems.answer: Liban's chance is 1 out of 30; no, he would not *definitely* be chosen – each week is a fresh independent draw and he might never be picked, though it becomes more likely over many weeks.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Liban's chance is 1 out of 30; no, he would not *definitely* be chosen – each week is a fresh independent draw and he might never be picked, though it becomes more likely over many weeks.

realProblems.prompt: Sumaya rolls a die 120 times. About how many times should she expect to roll a number less than 3 (that is, a 1 or a 2)? Show your working.
realProblems.answer: A 1 or 2 is 2 out of 6 = one third; expect one third of 120 = 40 times.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A 1 or 2 is 2 out of 6 = one third; expect one third of 120 = 40 times.

realProblems.prompt: A fair die is rolled 60 times. Predict about how many times each number should appear, and explain why the real result might be different.
realProblems.answer: 1) Each number: probability one sixth, so expect 60 ÷ 6 = 10 times each; real results wobble because chance varies from trial to trial.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Each number: probability one sixth, so expect 60 ÷ 6 = 10 times each; real results wobble because chance varies from trial to trial.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A fair die is rolled 60 times. Predict about how many times each number should appear, and explain why the real result might be different.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Each number: probability one sixth, so expect 60 ÷ 6 = 10 times each; real results wobble because chance varies from trial to trial.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Maryam flipped a coin 40 times and got 17 heads. Find her experimental probability of heads, and say whether the coin seems roughly fair.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 17 out of 40; one half would be 20 out of 40, so 17 is a little below but close – the coin seems roughly fair.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A bag has 12 marbles. It is certain you will draw a marble, it is unlikely you draw yellow, and red and blue are equally likely. Give one possible number of each colour and explain your reasoning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: One possible answer: 5 red, 5 blue, 1 green, 1 yellow (total

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A spinner has 8 equal sections numbered 1 to 8. Use chance words to describe: a) spinning an even number, b) spinning a number greater than 6, c) spinning a number less than 9.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: – red and blue equal, yellow unlikely at 1 out of 12; other answers are possible as long as red = blue and yellow is small.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Fair and Unfair Chance Experiments.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A chance experiment is any activity with more than one possible result, where you cannot say for sure what will happen each time. Flipping a coin, rolling a die, spinning a spinner, and drawing a marble from a bag are all chance experiments. The single results – heads, or a 4, or a red marble – are called outcomes, and each go of the experiment is called a trial. Experiments can be fair or unfair. In a fair experiment, every outcome has an equal chance of happening. A fair coin gives heads and tails each a…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Experimental Probability.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: So far we have worked out chances by thinking – this is called theoretical probability, because it comes from the theory, or the idea, of the experiment. But we can also find a probability by actually doing the experiment many times and counting what happens. This is called experimental probability. ⭐ Experimental Probability Rule Experimental probability = number of times the outcome happened divided by the total number of trials. The more trials you do, the closer your experimental probability usually gets to…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, run a pretend coin-flip experiment with you, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and – if you can – a coin and a die beside you. Chance is a subject you learn best by doing it. Flip the coin, roll the die, and watch what happens. If a result surprises you, that is wonderful – surprises are how we discover that chance does not always do what we expect. Every scientist who studies chance once sat exactly where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
I can use the words certain, likely, even chance, unlikely and impossible correctly.
I can place events on a probability scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).
I can write a simple probability as a fraction, and know it can also be a decimal or percentage.
I can work out the probability of an event using favourable outcomes ÷ total outcomes.
I can compare two events using more likely than, less likely than and equally likely as.
I can tell the difference between a fair experiment and an unfair (biased) one.
